text: $SPCX People keep talking about Tesla merging with SpaceX like it’s some kind of master plan. The truth is Musk already has all the flexibility he needs without ever combining them. He can shift his own capital, talent, and resources across his companies whenever he wants — it already functions like a mini-Berkshire Hathaway, just with rockets, EVs, satellites, AI, and robotics instead of insurance and railroads. They’re entrenched together because the missions overlap, the tech overlaps, and Musk sits at the center of all of it. But they should never be entwined together. A merger would create valuation chaos, regulatory cross‑contamination, national‑security conflicts, shareholder warfare, and nonstop accusations of corruption — real, implied, or assumed. Keeping Tesla, SpaceX, Starlink, xAI, and the rest separate is what protects them. Musk already gets the benefits of coordination without the liabilities of merging. Why change this structure. $SPCX, $TSLA, $ARKX & $SPCE

text: SpaceX criticized the European Union’s proposal for allocating satellite spectrum, arguing it could undermine the expansion of its Starlink services following its record IPO. The European Commission plans to offer one-third of its 2 GHz frequency band to non-EU companies, while reserving the rest for bloc-based operators, effectively limiting foreign firms from directly providing satellite services to European governments. SpaceX warned that the rules could leave Europe without effective direct-to-device satellite connectivity, as such services become more affordable with the expansion of Starlink’s satellite network. The company noted that US firms, including SpaceX and AST Spacemobile Inc., currently rely on partnerships with European telecom operators due to lack of spectrum rights. According to SpaceX, the proposed framework would split usable spectrum into “practically unusable subdivisions” and may conflict with international regulations and Europe’s economic realities. $SPCX $ASTS
label: evidence_analysis

text: $ASTS The chances of AST SpaceMobile winning Japan's $1 billion J-LEO award by the end of June 2026 are considered high, with their Rakuten Mobile-backed alliance emerging as one of two leading contenders.
